volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Two Bacchises (Section 3.3.428 - Section 3.5.526)

sections.label: Section 3.3.428
sections.original: ibi cursu luctando hásta disco pugilatu pila
sections.translation: There did they exercise themselves rather with running , wrestling , the quoit , the javelin , boxing , the ball , and leaping , rather than with harlots or with kissing ;

sections.label: Section 3.3.430
sections.original: ibi suam aetatem extendebant , non in latebrosis locis .
sections.translation: there did they prolong their lives [*] , and not in secret-lurking holes . Then , when from the hippodrome [*] and school of exercise you had returned home , clad in your belted frock [*] , upon a stool by your masters [*] would you sit ; and there , when you were reading your book , if you made a mistake in a single syllable , your skin would be made as spotted as your nurse ’ s gown [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.3.435
sections.original: Propter me haec nunc meo sodali dici discrucior miser ;
sections.translation: I ’ m sorely vexed , to my sorrow , that on my account these things should be said about my friend . In his innocence he incurs this suspicion for my sake .

sections.label: Section 3.3.437
sections.original: Alii , Lyde , nunc sunt mores .
sections.translation: The manners , Lydus , now are altered .

sections.label: Section 3.3.437b
sections.original: Id equidem ego certo scio .
sections.translation: That , for my part , I know full well . For formerly , a man used to receive public honors by the votes of the people , before he ceased to be obedient to one appointed his tutor .

sections.label: Section 3.3.440
sections.original: at nunc , prius quam septuennis est , si attingas eum manu ,
sections.translation: But now-a-days , before he is seven years old , if you touch a boy with your hand , at once the child breaks his tutor ’ s head with his tablet . When you go to complain to the father , thus says the father to the child : Be you my own dear boy , since you can defend yourself from an injury . The tutor then is called for ; Hallo ! you old good-for-nothing [*] ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.445
sections.original: ne attigas puerum istac causa , quando fecit strenue .”
sections.translation: don ’ t you be touching the child for this reason , that he has behaved so boldly . and thus the despised tutor becomes just like a lantern [*] with his oiled linen rags . Judgment pronounced , they go away thence . Can this preceptor then , on these terms , keep up his authority , if he himself is to be beaten the first ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.449
sections.original: Acris postulatio haec est . cum huius dicta intellego ,
sections.translation: This is a severe accusation . So far as I understand his words ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.450
sections.original: mira sunt ni Pistoclerus Lydum pugnis contudit .
sections.translation: ’ tis strange if Pistoclerus has never thumped Lydus with his fists .

sections.label: Section 3.3.451
sections.original: Sed quis hic est , quem astantem video ante ostium ? o Philoxene ,
sections.translation: But who is it that I see standing here before the door ? O Philoxenus , I would not prefer for myself to behold even the propitious Gods rather than him .

sections.label: Section 3.3.453
sections.original: Quis illic est ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s this ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.453b
sections.original: Mnesilochus , gnati tui sodalis Pistocleri .
sections.translation: Why , he ’ s Mnesilochus , the friend of your son . He is not of a like turn of mind with him who reclines and takes his meals in brothels .

sections.label: Section 3.3.455
sections.original: fortunatum Nicobulum , qui illum produxit sibi .
sections.translation: Fortunate Nicobulus ! who begot him for himself .

sections.label: Section 3.3.456
sections.original: Salvos sis , Mnesiloche , salvom te advenire gaudeo .
sections.translation: My greetings to you , Mnesilochus ; I ’ m glad that you have arrived safe .

sections.label: Section 3.3.457
sections.original: Di te ament , Philoxene .
sections.translation: May the Gods favour you , Philoxenus .

sections.label: Section 3.3.457b
sections.original: Hic enim rite productust patri :
sections.translation: He , now , was born at a lucky moment for his father ; he goes to sea , attends to the interests of his family , takes care of the house , is obedient and attentive to the wish and commands of his father .

sections.label: Section 3.3.460
sections.original: hic sodalis Pistoclero iam puer puero fuit ;
sections.translation: He , when but a boy , was the companion of Pistoclerus in his boyhood ; ’ tis not by three days that he is the older in age , but his disposition is more improved by thirty years than that of the other .

sections.label: Section 3.3.463
sections.original: Cave malo et compesce in illum dicere iniuste .
sections.translation: Beware of a mischief [*] , and forbear to speak of him unjustly .

sections.label: Section 3.3.463b
sections.original: Tace ,
sections.translation: Hold your peace . You ’ re a foolish man , who cannot bear to have him badly spoken of who does badly .

sections.label: Section 3.3.465
sections.original: nam illum meum malum promptare malim quam peculium .
sections.translation: But I would rather have him draw upon my mishaps than upon my savings [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.3.466
sections.original: Quidum ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.466b
sections.original: Quia , malum si promptet , in dies faciat minus .
sections.translation: Because , if he were to draw upon my mishaps , he would each day be making them less .

sections.label: Section 3.3.467
sections.original: Quid sodalem meum castigas , Lyde , discipulum tuom ?
sections.translation: Why , Lydus , are you censuring your pupil , my friend ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.468
sections.original: Periit tibi sodalis .
sections.translation: Your friend is ruined .

sections.label: Section 3.3.468b
sections.original: Ne di sirint .
sections.translation: May the Gods forbid it .

sections.label: Section 3.3.468c
sections.original: Sic est ut loquor .
sections.translation: ’ Tis so as I say . And further , I myself saw it when he was undone ; I am not censuring him from hearsay .

sections.label: Section 3.3.470
sections.original: Quid factum est ?
sections.translation: What has been done by him ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.470b
sections.original: Meretricem indigne deperit .
sections.translation: He is disgracefully doting upon a harlot .

sections.label: Section 3.3.470c
sections.original: Non tu taces ?
sections.translation: Won ’ t you be silent now ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.471
sections.original: Atque acerrume aestuosam : absorbet ubi quemque attigit .
sections.translation: She , too , like a tide , most voraciously swallows all up , whenever she has touched any one .

sections.label: Section 3.3.472
sections.original: Vbi ea mulier habitat ?
sections.translation: Where does this woman live ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.472b
sections.original: Hic .
sections.translation: Here .

sections.label: Section 3.3.472c
sections.original: Vnde eam esse aiunt ?
sections.translation: Whence do they say she comes ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.472d
sections.original: Ex Samo .
sections.translation: From Samos .

sections.label: Section 3.3.473
sections.original: Quae vocatur ?
sections.translation: What ’ s her name ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.473b
sections.original: Bacchis .
sections.translation: Bacchis .

sections.label: Section 3.3.473c
sections.original: Erras , Lyde : ego omnem rem scio
sections.translation: You are wrong , Lydus ; I know the whole affair , just as it is . You are blaming Pistoclerus without reason , and in his innocence .

sections.label: Section 3.3.475
sections.original: nam ille amico et benevolenti suo sodali sedulo
sections.translation: For he is carefully performing the business enjoined on him by his friend and companion , his sincere well-wisher . Neither is he himself in love , nor do you suppose him so .

sections.label: Section 3.3.477
sections.original: Itane oportet rem mandatam gerere amici sedulo ,
sections.translation: Is it necessary for him carefully to perform the business enjoined upon him by his friend in this fashion — for himself , sitting down , to hold a damsel in his lap who is kissing him ? Can the business thus entrusted be in no way transacted unless ever and anon

sections.label: Section 3.3.480
sections.original: manus ferát ei ad papillas , lábra ab labris nusquam auferat ?
sections.translation: he is placing his hand upon the bosom of Bacchis , or never withdraws his lips from hers ? But I ’ m ashamed to make mention of other things which I have seen him do ; when , in my presence , I saw him take most unbecoming liberties with the person of Bacchis , and yet not be at all ashamed . What need of words is there ? My pupil , your friend , his son ( pointing to PHILOXENUS ) , is ruined .

sections.label: Section 3.3.485
sections.original: nám ego illum periisse dico quoi quidem periit pudor .
sections.translation: For I say that he is ruined , whose modesty in fact is lost . What need of words is there ? Had I been willing to wait only a little time , that I might have had a better opportunity of viewing him , I then should , I think , have seen more than would have been proper for me to see , and for him to do .

sections.label: Section 3.3.489
sections.original: Perdidisti me , sodalis . egone ut illam mulierem
sections.translation: Friend , you have undone me . And ought I not to punish this woman with death ? I should prefer that I should perish after some dreadful fashion .

sections.label: Section 3.3.491
sections.original: satin ut quem tu habeás fidelem tibi aut cui credas néscias ?
sections.translation: Isn ’ t it the fact , you know not whom to deem faithful to yourself , or in whom to put your trust ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.492
sections.original: Viden ut aegre patitur gnatum ésse corruptum tuom ,
sections.translation: Don ’ t you see how much he grieves that your son , his friend , has been corrupted ? and how he is afflicting himself with sorrow ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.494
sections.original: Mnesiloche , hoc tecum oro , ut illius animum atque ingenium regas ;
sections.translation: Mnesilochus , I beg this of you , that you will influence his feelings and his disposition .

sections.label: Section 3.3.495
sections.original: serva tibi sodalem ét mihi filium .
sections.translation: Preserve for yourself a friend as well as a son for me .

sections.label: Section 3.3.495b
sections.original: Factum volo .
sections.translation: I fain would do so .

sections.label: Section 3.3.496
sections.original: Melius esset , me quoque una si cum illo relinqueres .
sections.translation: Much better , too , would you leave me here together with him .

sections.label: Section 3.3.497
sections.original: Adfatim est .
sections.translation: more than enough .

sections.label: Section 3.3.497b
sections.original: Mnesiloche , cura , ei , concastiga hominem probe ,
sections.translation: Mnesilochus has cares ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.498
sections.original: qui dedecorat te , me amicosque alios flagitiis suis .
sections.translation: Rate the man soundly , [*] who disgraces me , yourself his friend , and others , by his excesses .

sections.label: Section 3.3.499
sections.original: In te ego hoc onus omne impono . Lyde , sequere hac me .
sections.translation: Upon you do I impose all this responsibility . Lydus , follow me this way .

sections.label: Section 3.3.499b
sections.original: Sequor .—
sections.translation: I follow you .

sections.label: Section 3.4.500
sections.original: Inimiciorem nunc utrum credam magis
sections.translation: Which of the two now I should think to be my greater enemy , my companion or Bacchis , is extremely doubtful . Has she chosen him in preference ? Let her keep him , that ’ s the best . Surely , by my troth , she has done this to her own loss . For never let [*] any one entrust to me aught that is sacred ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.505
sections.original: ni ego illam exemplis plurumis planeque — amo .
sections.translation: if I don ’ t by an abundant example [*] , and — assuredly love her . I ’ ll make her not to say that she has got hold of a person to make a fool of . But I ’ ll go home now and pilfer something from my father . Even to such straits will I force her , that beggary shall be the lot of [*] — my father . But do I really now possess my wits with a mind unimpaired ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.510
sections.original: qui ad hunc modum haec hic quae futura fabulor ?
sections.translation: who am in this fashion prating here of these things that are to come to pass ? I ’ faith , I ’ m of opinion that I ’ m in love , inasmuch as I know for sure I am . But still , than that she , from my abundance , should , by the scraping of a single feather , grow the richer , I ’ d rather outvie a beggar in begging .

sections.label: Section 3.4.515
sections.original: numquam edepol viva me inridebit . nam mihi
sections.translation: Never , by Heaven , while I live , shall she make a laughing-stock of me . For I have made up my mind to pay down all the gold at once to my father . Upon me , therefore , needy and penniless , shall she fawn , at the time , when it shall be for her advantage not a whit the more than if she were saying her pretty things to a dead man at his tomb .

sections.label: Section 3.4.520
sections.original: profecto stabilest me patri aurum reddere .
sections.translation: Beyond a doubt , ’ tis my fixed determination to give the gold up to my father . Likewise , I ’ ll entreat that , for my sake , my father won ’ t hurt Chrysalus , nor censure him at all on my account with respect to the gold which he has deceived him about . For ’ tis right that I should have a care for him , who , for my sake ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.525
sections.original: mendacium éi dixit . vos me sequimini .—
sections.translation: has told this falsehood . ( To some ATTENDANTS ) Do you follow me .

sections.label: Section 3.5.526
sections.original: Rébus aliis antevortar , Bacchis , quae mandas mihi :
sections.translation: Before other matters , Bacchis , will I give place to what you enjoin me , that I find out Mnesilochus , and bring him , together with myself , to you . But at this my mind is surprised , if my messenger has reached him , what it is that delays him . I ’ ll go into his house here , and see if perchance he ’ s at home .
